understanding.goal_understanding: The goal of the game was to complete each level by getting a movable (pushable) object onto one or more goal tiles marked with '+', or otherwise triggering a WIN state when the level-specific success condition was satisfied.
understanding.mechanics_understanding: The world was laid out on a 2D grid with coordinates [x, y]. Some tiles were walls or obstacles that blocked movement. You controlled a currently selected piece. Movement actions (MOVE_LEFT, MOVE_RIGHT, MOVE_UP, MOVE_DOWN) attempted to move the selected piece one cell in the specified direction, but the move would fail (no position change) if blocked by walls, edges, or possibly other solid objects. In some situations, pieces could be pushed: moving into a tile occupied by a pushable object would push that object along, provided the space beyond it was free, allowing you to maneuver objects onto goal tiles. CLICK [x, y] actions changed which piece was selected: if a selectable piece existed at [x, y], the selected piece instantly switched to that one (or, in some levels, the selected piece teleported between defined linked positions, such as between [21, 21] and [42, 21]). Each level had a limited number of steps; every MOVE or CLICK consumed one step. When steps reached zero without satisfying the goal, the run ended without a WIN.

orient_history: Discarding prior hypothesis that MOVE alone could solve from current position. New hypothesis based only on observations: CLICK on a coordinate within another selectable changes which piece is currently selected (or teleports the current piece between existing selectables). Movement of the selected piece is constrained by walls shown in the movement report and semantic grid. Since P is trapped in a wall pocket, further MOVE_* will not help; I likely need to CLICK near or on the other selectable S at x≈7,y≈7 (since 21/3=7, 21/3=7) to shift control or position, then move that piece toward one of the goal regions, probably the right goal around x≈11,y≈5 (35/3≈11.7,17/3≈5.7) or left goal around x≈0,y≈7 (2/3≈0.7,23/3≈7.7). The only way to change which entity I control appears to be CLICK.

Discarding previous hypothesis. Based only on observation: (1) There are two selectables at [21,21] (S) and [42,21] (P). (2) CLICK on a selectable’s coordinates appears to change which one is selected and teleport the `player` between their positions; the clicked selectable becomes the new player position. (3) Movement for the right-hand selectable is fully blocked in all directions by walls/goal, so MOVE_* is useless here. (4) Goals are at [2,23] and [35,17]; neither selectable has ever moved relative to the walls/goals except via teleport swapping, so likely the win condition involves selecting or teleporting the correct piece relative to a goal, not physically walking into it. (5) With 2 steps left, the only meaningful different action type from MOVE is CLICK; clicking the other selectable may be the only way to change anything now.

Agent explained goal and mechanics.

understanding.goal_understanding: The goal of the game was to complete each level by getting a movable (pushable) object onto one or more goal tiles marked with '+', or otherwise triggering a WIN state when the level-specific success condition was satisfied.
understanding.mechanics_understanding: The world was laid out on a 2D grid with coordinates [x, y]. Some tiles were walls or obstacles that blocked movement. You controlled a currently selected piece. Movement actions (MOVE_LEFT, MOVE_RIGHT, MOVE_UP, MOVE_DOWN) attempted to move the selected piece one cell in the specified direction, but the move would fail (no position change) if blocked by walls, edges, or possibly other solid objects. In some situations, pieces could be pushed: moving into a tile occupied by a pushable object would push that object along, provided the space beyond it was free, allowing you to maneuver objects onto goal tiles. CLICK [x, y] actions changed which piece was selected: if a selectable piece existed at [x, y], the selected piece instantly switched to that one (or, in some levels, the selected piece teleported between defined linked positions, such as between [21, 21] and [42, 21]). Each level had a limited number of steps; every MOVE or CLICK consumed one step. When steps reached zero without satisfying the goal, the run ended without a WIN.
